title: WIDTH_BUCKET vs NTILE Decision Guide
when_to_use: user asks 'should I use equal ranges or equal counts for segmentation?'
building histograms where bins need equal width (WIDTH_BUCKET) vs equal observations (NTILE)
segmenting customers into quartiles where business wants 'top 25%' (NTILE) vs 'income $100k–$150k' (WIDTH_BUCKET)
designing dashboards where users click to see 'middle 50%' (NTILE) or 'scores 70–90' (WIDTH_BUCKET)
choosing between percentile-based and fixed-threshold buckets
when_not_to_use: when buckets must reflect real value clusters — use clustering or density-based methods, not WIDTH_BUCKET/NTILE
when data is highly skewed and equal-count buckets hide tail behavior — use WIDTH_BUCKET on log-transformed data instead
when exact quantiles matter (e.g., regulatory thresholds) — use PERCENTILE_CONT/PERCENTILE_DISC directly instead of NTILE
for time-series segmentation where buckets should adapt to recent data — use rolling quantiles instead of fixed NTILE
when only two buckets are needed — use CASE WHEN with median, not NTILE(2) or WIDTH_BUCKET(2)
when null values must be excluded — both functions handle NULLs differently; NTILE skips them, WIDTH_BUCKET treats them as outliers

concept_explanation.what_it_does: WIDTH_BUCKET assigns rows to bins of equal *value range* (e.g., $0–$100, $100–$200), while NTILE assigns rows to bins of equal *count* (e.g., top 25%, next 25%). WIDTH_BUCKET reflects data distribution; NTILE ensures balanced groups. They answer different business questions.
concept_explanation.mental_model: WIDTH_BUCKET is like cutting a ruler into equal-length segments — each segment covers $100 regardless of how many people fall in it. NTILE is like dealing cards evenly — each bucket gets the same number of cards, even if some ranges are denser. Ruler vs dealing.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: WIDTH_BUCKET(key, min, max, nbuckets) requires explicit min/max — if data exceeds range, it returns 0 or nbuckets+1. NTILE(n) on n rows returns bucket 1–n, but with remainder rows distributed one-per-bucket from left.

template.realistic:
-- Compare WIDTH_BUCKET vs NTILE for revenue segmentation
WITH sales_summary AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    SUM(order_total) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY 1
),
revenue_stats AS (
  SELECT
    MIN(revenue) AS min_rev,
    MAX(revenue) AS max_rev
  FROM sales_summary
),
width_segments AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    revenue,
    width_bucket(revenue, 0, 1000, 5) AS width_bucket -- $0–200, $200–400, ..., >1000
  FROM sales_summary
),
ntile_segments AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    revenue,
    ntile(5) OVER (ORDER BY revenue) AS ntile_bucket
  FROM sales_summary
)
SELECT
  'WIDTH_BUCKET' AS method,
  revenue_bucket AS segment,
  COUNT(*) AS customers,
  AVG(revenue) AS avg_revenue,
  MIN(revenue) AS min_rev,
  MAX(revenue) AS max_rev
FROM width_segments
WHERE revenue BETWEEN 0 AND 1000
GROUP BY 1, 2
UNION ALL
SELECT
  'NTILE' AS method,
  ntile_bucket AS segment,
  COUNT(*) AS customers,
  AVG(revenue) AS avg_revenue,
  MIN(revenue) AS min_rev,
  MAX(revenue) AS max_rev
FROM ntile_segments
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY method, segment;
template.composable:
-- Revenue segmentation for campaign targeting (combines seg_width_bucket_ntile_guide + seg_case_when_classification)
-- Composes: seg_width_bucket_ntile_guide + seg_case_when_classification
WITH sales_summary AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    SUM(order_total) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY 1
),
revenue_stats AS (
  SELECT
    MIN(revenue) AS min_rev,
    MAX(revenue) AS max_rev
  FROM sales_summary
),
combined AS (
  SELECT
    s.customer_id,
    s.revenue,
    -- WIDTH_BUCKET for value-range segments
    CASE
      WHEN width_bucket(s.revenue, COALESCE(rs.min_rev, 0), COALESCE(rs.max_rev, 10000), 10) BETWEEN 1 AND 3 THEN 'low_value'
      WHEN width_bucket(s.revenue, COALESCE(rs.min_rev, 0), COALESCE(rs.max_rev, 10000), 10) BETWEEN 4 AND 7 THEN 'mid_value'
      ELSE 'high_value'
    END AS range_segment,
    -- NTILE for population segments
    CASE
      WHEN ntile(4) OVER (ORDER BY s.revenue) = 1 THEN 'bottom_quartile'
      WHEN ntile(4) OVER (ORDER BY s.revenue) = 2 THEN 'second_quartile'
      WHEN ntile(4) OVER (ORDER BY s.revenue) = 3 THEN 'third_quartile'
      ELSE 'top_quartile'
    END AS population_segment
  FROM sales_summary s
  CROSS JOIN revenue_stats rs
)
SELECT
  range_segment,
  population_segment,
  COUNT(*) AS customers,
  AVG(revenue) AS avg_revenue,
  ROUND(STDDEV(revenue), 2) AS stddev_revenue
FROM combined
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY range_segment, population_segment

mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If a partition has fewer rows than NTILE(n), some buckets are empty. E.g., NTILE(4) on 2 rows yields buckets 1 and 2 only — no 3 or 4. Users expect quartiles but see gaps.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Segment counts don't add up; charts show 'missing' segments. Queries filtering ntile_bucket = 4 return no rows even though data exists.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT customer_id, ntile(4) OVER (ORDER BY revenue) AS quartile FROM top_customers WHERE region = 'West' AND COUNT(*) < 4;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT customer_id, NTILE(GREATEST(1, (SELECT LEAST(4, COUNT(*)) FROM top_customers WHERE region = 'West'))) OVER (ORDER BY revenue) AS quartile FROM top_customers WHERE region = 'West';
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
-- Check if any bucket is missing:
WITH buckets AS (
  SELECT ntile(4) OVER (ORDER BY revenue) AS quartile FROM top_customers WHERE region = 'West'
)
SELECT GENERATE_SERIES(1, 4) AS expected_bucket
EXCEPT
SELECT quartile FROM buckets;

mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: WIDTH_BUCKET(NULL, min, max, nbuckets) returns NULL. If the key column has NULLs, those rows get no bucket — silent exclusion from analysis.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Row count in segments is less than total rows; NULLs are invisible in output.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT width_bucket(revenue, 0, 1000, 5) AS rev_bucket FROM sales;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sales WHERE revenue IS NULL;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT width_bucket(revenue, 0, 1000, 5) AS rev_bucket FROM sales) WHERE rev_bucket IS NULL;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Mixing min/max in WIDTH_BUCKET with actual data range
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If data contains values beyond [min, max], they map to bucket 0 or nbuckets+1. Users often miss this and think their high-value customers are 'out of range' instead of bucketed.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Unexpected bucket 0 or bucket 6 (for 5 buckets) — users can't interpret these values.
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
-- Find rows outside expected range:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sales WHERE revenue < 0 OR revenue > 1000;

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: Built-in function: width_bucket(value, min, max, num_buckets). Returns 1–nbuckets or 0 and nbuckets+1 for outliers.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Not supported — throws error. Use a CTE with WIDTH_BUCKET emulation or PERCENT_RANK.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Not supported — error. Use NTILE, width_bucket emulation, or CASE WHEN with PRECENTILE_CONT.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: In BigQuery, use NTILE or PERCENT_RANK. For equal-width buckets, compute range boundaries: MIN + (MAX-MIN)/nbuckets * bucket_id.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: NTILE works with PARTITION BY — each partition gets its own buckets. If a partition has fewer rows than nbuckets, some buckets are empty.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same — but older versions have bugs with PARTITION BY in NTILE; test first.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Supported and stable. Handles partitions correctly.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Always verify bucket distribution per partition: SELECT partition_col, ntile_bucket, COUNT(*) GROUP BY 1,2. If any count=0, consider adaptive bucketing.

choose_this_when: user wants segments where *every bucket has roughly the same number of customers* — use NTILE
user wants segments based on *fixed value ranges* (e.g., $0–$100, $100–$200) — use WIDTH_BUCKET
building marketing campaigns targeting 'top quartile' (NTILE) vs 'spenders $500+ (WIDTH_BUCKET)

choose_alternative_when.why: PERCENTILE_CONT gives exact quantiles; NTILE only approximates them with integer buckets

choose_alternative_when.condition: user needs adaptive thresholds that change with data distribution (e.g., quarterly rebased)
choose_alternative_when.why: Dynamic thresholds recompute from recent data; NTILE/width_bucket use static min/max or fixed quantiles

edge_cases.scenario: Zero variance in key column (all values identical)
edge_cases.what_happens: WIDTH_BUCKET returns 1 for all rows (min=max, so only one bin fits all). NTILE returns 1 for all rows if nbuckets > count, or distributes 1–n if nbuckets <= count.
edge_cases.handling: Check variance first: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT key) FROM table. If 1, skip segmentation or use a different dimension.

edge_cases.what_happens: WIDTH_BUCKET over-represents low ranges; NTILE splits the long tail into tiny buckets. Both hide the actual distribution.
edge_cases.handling: Transform the key before segmentation: WIDTH_BUCKET(LOG10(revenue + 1), 0, MAX_LOG, nbuckets). Or use log-spaced bins manually.

edge_cases.scenario: Data updates change min/max significantly between runs
edge_cases.what_happens: WIDTH_BUCKET buckets shift meaning — today's bucket 3 ($100–$200) might be $500–$1000 tomorrow. NTILE buckets stay stable relative to current data.
edge_cases.handling: Use NTILE for dynamic systems where thresholds shouldn’t re-baseline. Or compute min/max from a fixed training period (e.g., last 90 days) for WIDTH_BUCKET.

performance_notes.complexity: WIDTH_BUCKET: O(n) for scan + O(1) per row; NTILE: O(n log n) for sort + O(n) scan
performance_notes.when_slow: NTILE on large partitions (>10M rows) — sort dominates. WIDTH_BUCKET is fast but slow if min/max computed with subqueries on huge tables.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Cache min/max in a CTE for WIDTH_BUCKET. Pre-aggregate partitions in CTEs before applying NTILE. Use EXPLAIN to verify DuckDB uses index scans where possible.

title: CASE WHEN Classification
when_to_use: user needs to map metrics to business categories (e.g., 'High', 'Medium', 'Low' risk)
classifying customers by RFM scores or rule-based tiers
segmenting products by margin, growth, or strategic importance
flagging anomalies where simple thresholds apply (e.g., >1000 = 'outlier')
encoding business rules into analytical queries for dashboards
when_not_to_use: when more than 100 distinct categories — use a lookup table or join instead
when thresholds should adapt to data distribution — use dynamic thresholds via PERCENTILE_CONT instead
when logic must change per segment size — use NTILE or WIDTH_BUCKET
for time-series thresholds (e.g., moving z-scores) — use window functions with dynamic baselines
when rules are too complex for SQL — offload to application layer or ML model

concept_explanation.what_it_does: CASE WHEN evaluates conditions in order and returns the first matching value. It’s the most common way to encode business rules directly in SQL — e.g., IF score > 80 THEN 'Gold' ELSE 'Silver'. Its ordered nature means top-down priority matters.
concept_explanation.mental_model: It’s like reading a decision tree from top to bottom. The first rule that's TRUE wins — others are skipped. If no rule matches, ELSE provides a fallback. It’s a prioritized if-else chain.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: CASE WHEN evaluates *in order*. Placing a broad condition (e.g., revenue > 0) before a narrow one (revenue > 1000) will cause the narrow rule to never trigger. Always order from most-specific to least-specific.

template.minimal:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  revenue,
  CASE
    WHEN revenue > 10000 THEN 'Platinum'
    WHEN revenue > 5000 THEN 'Gold'
    WHEN revenue > 1000 THEN 'Silver'
    ELSE 'Bronze'
  END AS tier
FROM customers;
template.realistic:
-- Customer segmentation for marketing campaigns based on RFM-like scores
WITH customer_scores AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    SUM(CASE WHEN order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days' THEN order_total ELSE 0 END) AS recency_score,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS frequency_score,
    SUM(order_total) AS monetary_score
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY 1
),
classified AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    recency_score,
    frequency_score,
    monetary_score,
    CASE
      WHEN recency_score > 500 AND frequency_score > 10 AND monetary_score > 2000 THEN 'Champion'
      WHEN recency_score > 500 AND frequency_score > 5 THEN 'Loyal'
      WHEN recency_score > 500 THEN 'Recent'
      WHEN frequency_score > 5 THEN 'Active'
      WHEN monetary_score > 500 THEN 'Spender'
      ELSE 'At Risk'
    END AS segment,
    CASE
      WHEN recency_score > 500 THEN 'High engagement'
      WHEN recency_score > 100 THEN 'Medium engagement'
      ELSE 'Low engagement'
    END AS engagement
  FROM customer_scores
)
SELECT
  segment,
  engagement,
  COUNT(*) AS customers,
  AVG(monetary_score) AS avg_monetary,
  SUM(monetary_score) AS total_revenue
FROM classified
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
template.composable:
-- Campaign prioritization (combines seg_case_when_classification + dq_time_filter_before_window)
-- Composes: seg_case_when_classification + dq_time_filter_before_window
WITH customer_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    SUM(CASE WHEN order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' THEN order_total ELSE 0 END) AS recent_revenue,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' THEN order_id END) AS recent_orders,
    SUM(CASE WHEN order_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days' THEN order_total ELSE 0 END) AS legacy_revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'
  GROUP BY 1
),
with_classification AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    recent_revenue,
    recent_orders,
    legacy_revenue,
    CASE
      WHEN recent_revenue > 500 THEN 'High_priority'
      WHEN recent_orders > 3 THEN 'Medium_priority'
      WHEN legacy_revenue > 1000 THEN 'Legacy_interest'
      ELSE 'Low_priority'
    END AS campaign_priority
  FROM customer_metrics
)
SELECT
  campaign_priority,
  COUNT(*) AS customers,
  ROUND(AVG(recent_revenue), 2) AS avg_recent_revenue
FROM with_classification
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY avg_recent_revenue DESC

mistake_patterns.mistake: Ordering CASE WHEN from least-specific to most-specific
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If a condition like revenue > 0 appears before revenue > 1000, the first condition matches *all* rows — subsequent rules never trigger. The query silently implements wrong logic.
mistake_patterns.symptom: All rows receive the same category (the first one), or high-value segments are underrepresented.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN revenue > 0 THEN 'All' WHEN revenue > 1000 THEN 'High' END FROM data;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN revenue > 1000 THEN 'High' WHEN revenue > 0 THEN 'All' END FROM data;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT revenue, CASE WHEN revenue > 0 THEN 'All' WHEN revenue > 1000 THEN 'High' END AS cat FROM data GROUP BY 1,2 HAVING COUNT(*) > 0 ORDER BY revenue;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using ELSE too early — it short-circuits subsequent rules
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Placing ELSE before all specific conditions makes all rows fall into ELSE. CASE WHEN stops at the first match, so anything after ELSE is ignored.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Specific categories never appear, even when data supports them.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN revenue > 500 THEN 'Mid' ELSE 'Low' WHEN revenue > 1000 THEN 'High' END FROM data;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN revenue > 1000 THEN 'High' WHEN revenue > 500 THEN 'Mid' ELSE 'Low' END FROM data;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM data WHERE revenue > 1000;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (above query) WHERE cat = 'High'; -- Should match above count

mistake_patterns.mistake: Assuming CASE WHEN handles NULLs like zero
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: NULL comparisons in CASE WHEN return NULL (not TRUE/FALSE), so rules with NULL conditionals never trigger unless explicitly handled. E.g., revenue > 1000 is NULL when revenue is NULL — falls through to ELSE.
mistake_patterns.symptom: NULL values in key columns get classified as 'Other' or the first ELSE match, hiding data issues.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN revenue > 1000 THEN 'High' ELSE 'Other' END FROM data;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN revenue IS NULL THEN 'Missing' WHEN revenue > 1000 THEN 'High' ELSE 'Other' END FROM data;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM data WHERE revenue IS NULL;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (above query) WHERE cat = 'Missing';

dialect_traps.construct: CASE WHEN in DuckDB vs other dialects
dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: Full CASE WHEN support — order-sensitive, NULL-safe comparisons require explicit IS NULL checks. CASE returns first matching condition.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Identical behavior — same SQL syntax and semantics.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: CASE WHEN supported, but BigQuery’s type coercion may infer unexpected types — cast explicitly to avoid errors.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Always use IS NULL for null checks. Explicitly order rules from specific to general. In BigQuery, add CAST around CASE for safety.

dialect_traps.construct: Multiple CASE WHEN in same SELECT
dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: Each CASE is independent — no performance penalty for repeating similar logic. DuckDB may share computed expressions internally.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same — but complex CASE WHEN may be slower than CTE-based pre-computation.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same, but BigQuery may bill for each CASE expression separately in complex queries.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: If CASE WHEN logic is repeated across many columns, compute once in a CTE and reference: WITH classified AS (SELECT ..., CASE(...) AS segment), then reference segment elsewhere.

choose_this_when: user says 'if X then Y, else Z' — clear business rules
building prioritized rules where order matters (e.g., 'Champion' > 'Loyal' > 'At Risk')
encoding legacy system logic into analytics queries

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants equal-sized segments regardless of values
choose_alternative_when.why: NTILE/width_bucket enforce balance; CASE WHEN depends on data distribution and may create imbalanced buckets

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants thresholds that adapt to recent data distribution
choose_alternative_when.why: PERCENTILE-based thresholds rebase automatically; hard-coded CASE WHEN thresholds become stale

edge_cases.what_happens: The first matching rule wins — revenue > 500 catches both $600 and $2000, so the second rule is never reached.
edge_cases.handling: Order rules from most-specific to least-specific: revenue > 1000, revenue > 500, revenue > 0, else Other.

edge_cases.what_happens: Comparison with NULL returns NULL, so rules fail. ELSE catches NULLs, but users may not realize data is missing.
edge_cases.handling: Add explicit IS NULL checks at the top of CASE WHEN: CASE WHEN key IS NULL THEN 'Missing' WHEN key > 1000 THEN 'High' END.

edge_cases.scenario: Data drift changes distribution over time
edge_cases.what_happens: Thresholds that worked last quarter no longer segment correctly — e.g., $1000 used to be top 10% but now is top 50%.
edge_cases.handling: Recompute thresholds quarterly using PERCENTILE_CONT and hard-code those numbers, or switch to NTILE for dynamic segmentation.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) per row — CASE WHEN is evaluated row-by-row, but no additional scans
performance_notes.when_slow: Very long CASE WHEN chains (>15 conditions) in large tables — CPU-bound but rarely the bottleneck.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Move frequent conditions to the top. If multiple CASE WHEN use the same input, compute once in a CTE. Use EXPLAIN to verify DuckDB doesn’t recompute expressions.

title: Dynamic Thresholds via Percentile
when_to_use: user needs thresholds that adapt to data distribution (e.g., 'top 20%' of current data)
avoiding hardcoded thresholds that become outdated as data evolves
segmenting by quartiles/quintiles where exact values aren’t business-defined
detecting anomalies relative to recent distribution (e.g., 'above 95th percentile')
building reports where segments should rebase automatically each period
when_not_to_use: when business rules require fixed thresholds (e.g., regulatory: 'income > $100k' is always high)
when data has outliers that distort percentiles — use IQR or capped thresholds instead
when segments must be stable over time for comparison — dynamic thresholds break trend consistency
for time-series where rolling windows are needed — use moving percentile approximations
when percentile computation is too expensive for frequent re-computation (e.g., real-time)

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Dynamic thresholds compute percentile cutoffs from current data (e.g., 25th, 50th, 75th percentiles) and use them to assign segments. Instead of 'revenue > 1000', it says 'revenue in top 25% of this month'. This ensures segments reflect current data distribution.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine measuring everyone’s height and cutting a histogram into quarters — today’s 'short' person might be 'tall' next year. Percentile-based thresholds rebaseline automatically; fixed thresholds don’t.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: PERCENTILE_CONT interpolates values (e.g., 50th percentile = median), while PERCENTILE_DISC picks an actual value. PERCENTILE_CONT gives smoother thresholds; PERCENTILE_DISC is discrete and safer for categorization.

template.minimal:
-- Compute 25th, 50th, 75th percentiles once
WITH stats AS (
  SELECT
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p25,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p50,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p75
  FROM sales
)
SELECT
  customer_id,
  revenue,
  CASE
    WHEN revenue > p75 THEN 'top_quartile'
    WHEN revenue > p50 THEN 'second_quartile'
    WHEN revenue > p25 THEN 'third_quartile'
    ELSE 'bottom_quartile'
  END AS segment
FROM sales, stats;
template.realistic:
-- Dynamic customer segments by revenue percentiles
WITH monthly_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    SUM(order_total) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE order_date >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE) - INTERVAL '1 month'
  GROUP BY 1
),
percentiles AS (
  SELECT
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p25,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p50,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p75
  FROM monthly_revenue
),
classified AS (
  SELECT
    r.customer_id,
    r.revenue,
    CASE
      WHEN r.revenue > p.p75 THEN 'top_quartile'
      WHEN r.revenue > p.p50 THEN 'second_quartile'
      WHEN r.revenue > p.p25 THEN 'third_quartile'
      ELSE 'bottom_quartile'
    END AS segment
  FROM monthly_revenue r, percentiles p
)
SELECT
  segment,
  COUNT(*) AS customers,
  ROUND(AVG(revenue), 2) AS avg_revenue,
  ROUND(STDDEV(revenue), 2) AS stddev_revenue
FROM classified
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY avg_revenue DESC;
template.composable:
-- Dynamic anomaly detection: flag values > 2σ above mean (combines seg_dynamic_thresholds + stat_z_score_detection)
-- Composes: seg_dynamic_thresholds + stat_z_score_detection
WITH revenue_stats AS (
  SELECT
    AVG(revenue) AS mean_rev,
    STDDEV(revenue) AS stddev_rev
  FROM monthly_revenue
),
percentiles AS (
  SELECT
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.025) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p2_5,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.975) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p97_5
  FROM monthly_revenue
),
classified AS (
  SELECT
    r.customer_id,
    r.revenue,
    s.mean_rev,
    s.stddev_rev,
    CASE
      WHEN r.revenue > s.mean_rev + 2 * s.stddev_rev THEN 'above_upper_bound'
      WHEN r.revenue < s.mean_rev - 2 * s.stddev_rev THEN 'below_lower_bound'
      ELSE 'normal'
    END AS anomaly_flag,
    CASE
      WHEN r.revenue > p.p97_5 THEN 'top_2_5_pct'
      WHEN r.revenue < p.p2_5 THEN 'bottom_2_5_pct'
      ELSE 'middle_95_pct'
    END AS percentile_segment
  FROM monthly_revenue r, revenue_stats s, percentiles p
)
SELECT * FROM classified
WHERE anomaly_flag != 'normal' OR percentile_segment IN ('top_2_5_pct', 'bottom_2_5_pct')
ORDER BY revenue DESC

mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: PERCENTILE_CONT requires numeric input. If passed strings or dates, it returns an error or coerces to NaN.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query fails with 'function percentile_cont(numeric, numeric) does not exist' or returns NULL.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY name) FROM customers;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY CAST(revenue AS FLOAT)) FROM sales;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Not caching percentiles in a CTE before reuse
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If PERCENTILE_CONT is called multiple times in the same query, it recomputes — potentially returning slightly different values due to floating-point variance, breaking consistency.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Percentile-based conditions disagree with each other (e.g., revenue > p75 but < p25 in some rows).
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
SELECT
  CASE WHEN revenue > PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) THEN 'high' END,
  CASE WHEN revenue < PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) THEN 'low' END
FROM sales;
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
WITH stats AS (
  SELECT
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p25,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p75
  FROM sales
)
SELECT
  CASE WHEN revenue > p75 THEN 'high' END,
  CASE WHEN revenue < p25 THEN 'low' END
FROM sales, stats;

mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Percentiles adapt to current data — a threshold that was 'top 5%' last month might be 'top 50%' this month. Users comparing trends see apparent 'growth' due to rebaselining, not real improvement.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Segment counts or average metrics shift with no operational change — only data distribution changed.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
-- Compare top_quartile count this month vs last month using dynamic thresholds:
-- WRONG: each month’s quartiles rebase, so 'top' means different absolute values
WITH monthly AS (SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p75 FROM orders WHERE DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) = '2024-05-01'), ...
mistake_patterns.good_sql: To compare trends, use fixed thresholds: PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP over a fixed reference period (e.g., last 12 months).
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
-- Compute month-over-month p75 change — if >10%, re-baselining may distort trends
WITH m1 AS (SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p75 FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2024-04-01' AND order_date < '2024-05-01'),
m2 AS (SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p75 FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2024-05-01' AND order_date < '2024-06-01')
SELECT (m2.p75 - m1.p75) / NULLIF(m1.p75, 0) * 100 AS p75_change_pct FROM m1, m2;

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) works natively in DuckDB.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Supported since v14 — earlier versions use ARRAY_AGG + PERCENT_RANK. Postgres v13 and earlier lack WITHIN GROUP — use alternative.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Unsupported. BigQuery uses PERCENTILE_CONT(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY revenue ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING) + QUALIFY rn = 1, or use APPROX_PERCENTILES.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: In BigQuery, use PERCENTILE_CONT with OVER and filter to n/2 row. In older Postgres, compute percentiles with CTE + ROW_NUMBER.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: PERCENTILE_CONT ignores NULLs — they’re excluded from the sorted list. If all values are NULL, it returns NULL.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same — PERCENTILE_CONT skips NULLs. APPROX_PERCENTILES does not support NULLs at all.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Filter NULLs explicitly before percentile calculation: WHERE revenue IS NOT NULL. Or use COALESCE(revenue, 0) if zero is appropriate.

choose_this_when: user says 'segment by top 25%' instead of 'spend > $1000'
business wants segments that rebaseline automatically each period
data has outliers — fixed thresholds fail; percentiles are robust
comparing segments across time — each period’s thresholds adapt

choose_alternative_when.condition: business mandates fixed thresholds (e.g., 'income > $100k' is always high)
choose_alternative_when.why: Fixed thresholds ensure consistency across time; dynamic thresholds obscure absolute changes

choose_alternative_when.condition: user needs stable segment labels for trend analysis
choose_alternative_when.why: Dynamic thresholds change label meanings over time; fixed thresholds let 'Gold' always mean >$1000

choose_alternative_when.condition: data is sparse or non-normal — percentiles are misleading
choose_alternative_when.why: IQR (interquartile range) is more robust to outliers and sparse data than percentile-based thresholds

edge_cases.scenario: Data has few distinct values (e.g., 5 unique revenues)
edge_cases.what_happens: Percentiles collapse — p25, p50, p75 may all equal the same value. Segments become meaningless or empty.
edge_cases.handling: Check distinct count before computing: IF (COUNT(DISTINCT revenue) < 10) THEN use WIDTH_BUCKET or CASE WHEN instead.

edge_cases.what_happens: PERCENTILE_CONT returns a value based on non-NULLs — but business may expect NULLs treated as a segment (e.g., 'missing' category).
edge_cases.handling: Explicitly bucket NULLs first: CASE WHEN revenue IS NULL THEN 'Unknown' WHEN revenue > ... END.

edge_cases.scenario: Very skewed data (e.g., 1% of accounts have >90% of revenue)
edge_cases.what_happens: Percentiles are pulled by the long tail — 'top quartile' includes most revenue-generating accounts, but median is low.
edge_cases.handling: Use log-transformed revenue for percentile computation, or use IQR-based thresholds instead.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n log n) for sort + O(1) for percentile lookup
performance_notes.when_slow: Large tables (>100M rows) with PERCENTILE_CONT — the sort dominates. Re-computing percentiles frequently in ad-hoc queries adds latency.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-compute percentiles in a materialized view or CTE. For frequent queries, store monthly/quarterly percentiles in a lookup table. Use APPROX_PERCENTILES in DuckDB (optional) for speed vs accuracy tradeoff.

title: Multi-Dimensional Scoring
when_to_use: user needs to combine multiple metrics into one score (e.g., RFM: recency, frequency, monetary)
segmenting entities by composite criteria (e.g., 'high value customers' = high recency + high spend)
ranking products by a hybrid score (growth + margin + novelty)
building dashboards where simple KPIs are insufficient — users want 'one number' summary
prioritizing leads, patients, or cases where multiple dimensions matter
when_not_to_use: when dimensions are on incomparable scales — normalization is required, or use non-linear scaling
when dimensions conflict (e.g., high growth but low profitability) — consider separate scores instead
for regulatory scoring where formula must be auditable — document weights and avoid black-box models
when weights are arbitrary — use business-driven weights or regression coefficients instead
for time-series scoring where trends matter more than snapshots — use rolling scores instead

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Multi-dimensional scoring normalizes each metric (e.g., z-score or min-max), applies business weights, sums them into a composite score, and classifies entities into tiers (e.g., Platinum, Gold). It converts complex data into actionable segments.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine a dashboard with three dials (recency, frequency, spend) — each dial has 0–100, you weight them (recency 40%, spend 30%, frequency 30%), add them up, and the final number determines the tier. It’s a weighted average of normalized dials.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: Weights must sum to 1 (or 100%) for interpretability. Normalization ensures no single dimension dominates. Outliers can distort z-scores — consider capped normalization or IQR scaling.

template.minimal:
-- RFM scoring with z-score normalization and equal weights
WITH rfm AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('day', MAX(order_date)) AS last_order,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS frequency,
    SUM(order_total) AS monetary
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY 1
),
rfm_scaled AS (
  SELECT
    r.customer_id,
    -- Recency: days since last order (lower = better) → scale 0–100
    ROUND(100 - (EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (CURRENT_DATE - r.last_order)) / 86400 / NULLIF((SELECT MAX(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (CURRENT_DATE - last_order)))/86400 FROM rfm), 0) * 100), 0) AS r_score,
    -- Frequency: normalize 0–100
    ROUND(100 * r.frequency / NULLIF((SELECT MAX(frequency) FROM rfm), 0), 0) AS f_score,
    -- Monetary: normalize 0–100
    ROUND(100 * r.monetary / NULLIF((SELECT MAX(monetary) FROM rfm), 0), 0) AS m_score
  FROM rfm r
)
SELECT
  customer_id,
  r_score,
  f_score,
  m_score,
  ROUND(0.4 * r_score + 0.3 * f_score + 0.3 * m_score, 0) AS rfm_score,
  CASE
    WHEN (r_score + f_score + m_score) >= 240 THEN 'Platinum'
    WHEN (r_score + f_score + m_score) >= 180 THEN 'Gold'
    WHEN (r_score + f_score + m_score) >= 120 THEN 'Silver'
    ELSE 'Bronze'
  END AS tier
FROM rfm_scaled;
template.realistic:
-- RFM scoring with z-score normalization (handles outliers better than min-max)
WITH rfm AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('day', MAX(order_date)) AS last_order,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS frequency,
    SUM(order_total) AS monetary
  FROM orders
  WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'
  GROUP BY 1
),
rfm_stats AS (
  SELECT
    AVG(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (CURRENT_DATE - last_order))/86400) AS avg_recency,
    STDDEV(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (CURRENT_DATE - last_order))/86400) AS stddev_recency,
    AVG(frequency) AS avg_frequency,
    STDDEV(frequency) AS stddev_frequency,
    AVG(monetary) AS avg_monetary,
    STDDEV(monetary) AS stddev_monetary
  FROM rfm
),
rfm_z AS (
  SELECT
    r.customer_id,
    -- Recency: lower is better, so reverse z-score (higher = more recent)
    ROUND(
      50 + 20 * ((SELECT avg_recency FROM rfm_stats) - EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (CURRENT_DATE - r.last_order))/86400)
      / NULLIF((SELECT stddev_recency FROM rfm_stats), 0),
    0) AS r_z,
    -- Frequency: higher is better
    ROUND(
      50 + 20 * (r.frequency - (SELECT avg_frequency FROM rfm_stats))
      / NULLIF((SELECT stddev_frequency FROM rfm_stats), 0),
    0) AS f_z,
    -- Monetary: higher is better
    ROUND(
      50 + 20 * (r.monetary - (SELECT avg_monetary FROM rfm_stats))
      / NULLIF((SELECT stddev_monetary FROM rfm_stats), 0),
    0) AS m_z
  FROM rfm r
),
rfm_scored AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    r_z,
    f_z,
    m_z,
    ROUND(0.4 * r_z + 0.3 * f_z + 0.3 * m_z, 0) AS rfm_score
  FROM rfm_z
)
SELECT
  customer_id,
  rfm_score,
  CASE
    WHEN rfm_score >= 90 THEN 'Champion'
    WHEN rfm_score >= 70 THEN 'Loyal'
    WHEN rfm_score >= 50 THEN 'Potential'
    WHEN rfm_score >= 30 THEN 'At Risk'
    ELSE 'Lost'
  END AS tier
FROM rfm_scored
ORDER BY rfm_score DESC;
template.composable:
-- Campaign prioritization with multi-dimensional scoring (combines seg_multi_dimensional_scoring + dq_time_filter_before_window)
-- Composes: seg_multi_dimensional_scoring + dq_time_filter_before_window
WITH customer_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    SUM(CASE WHEN order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' THEN order_total ELSE 0 END) AS recent_spend,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' THEN order_id END) AS recent_orders,
    SUM(CASE WHEN order_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days' THEN order_total ELSE 0 END) AS legacy_spend
  FROM orders
  WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'
  GROUP BY 1
),
stats AS (
  SELECT
    AVG(recent_spend) AS avg_recent_spend,
    STDDEV(recent_spend) AS stddev_recent_spend,
    AVG(recent_orders) AS avg_recent_orders,
    STDDEV(recent_orders) AS stddev_recent_orders
  FROM customer_metrics
),
with_scores AS (
  SELECT
    cm.customer_id,
    cm.recent_spend,
    cm.recent_orders,
    cm.legacy_spend,
    -- Normalize and score (weights: recent_spend 60%, recent_orders 40%)
    ROUND(
      50 + 20 * (cm.recent_spend - s.avg_recent_spend)
      / NULLIF(s.stddev_recent_spend, 0),
    0) AS spend_score,
    ROUND(
      50 + 20 * (cm.recent_orders - s.avg_recent_orders)
      / NULLIF(s.stddev_recent_orders, 0),
    0) AS order_score,
    ROUND(
      0.6 * (50 + 20 * (cm.recent_spend - s.avg_recent_spend)
        / NULLIF(s.stddev_recent_spend, 0))
      + 0.4 * (50 + 20 * (cm.recent_orders - s.avg_recent_orders)
        / NULLIF(s.stddev_recent_orders, 0)),
    0) AS priority_score
  FROM customer_metrics cm, stats s
)
SELECT
  customer_id,
  priority_score,
  CASE
    WHEN priority_score >= 90 THEN 'Tier 1 - High Priority'
    WHEN priority_score >= 70 THEN 'Tier 2 - Medium Priority'
    ELSE 'Tier 3 - Low Priority'
  END AS campaign_tier
FROM with_scores
WHERE priority_score IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY priority_score DESC

mistake_patterns.mistake: Not normalizing before weighting — one dimension dominates
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If revenue ranges from $1–$10,000 and frequency is 1–100, revenue’s weight swamps frequency even with equal weights. The score reflects mostly revenue, not a true composite.
mistake_patterns.symptom: All high-value customers cluster at the top, low-value customers are indistinguishable; score lacks granularity.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT customer_id, 0.5 * revenue + 0.5 * frequency AS score FROM rfm;
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  0.5 * (revenue - min_rev) / NULLIF(max_rev - min_rev, 0) +
  0.5 * (frequency - min_freq) / NULLIF(max_freq - min_freq, 0)
  AS score
FROM rfm, stats;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT
  MAX(revenue), MIN(revenue),
  MAX(frequency), MIN(frequency)
FROM rfm;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Weights don’t sum to 1 — scores are uninterpretable
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Weights like 40+30+30=100 scale to 1.0, but 0.4+0.3+0.3=1.0 is correct. If weights sum to 2, the score is double what it should be.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Scores appear inflated; thresholds become arbitrary.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT customer_id, 0.4 * r_z + 0.3 * f_z + 0.3 * m_z + 0.3 * z_score2 AS score FROM rfm_z;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT customer_id, 0.4 * r_z + 0.3 * f_z + 0.3 * m_z AS score FROM rfm_z;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT SUM(weight) FROM (VALUES (0.4, 'r'), (0.3, 'f'), (0.3, 'm')) AS weights(w, name); -- Should be 1.0

mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Z-scores > 3 or < -3 are rare (0.3% of data). If not handled, a few outliers dominate the score. Capping at ±3 is common but loses information.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Top/bottom segments are driven by 1–2 extreme values; most customers cluster in middle tiers.
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE z > 3) AS z_gt_3,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE z < -3) AS z_lt_neg3
FROM (SELECT (value - mean) / stddev AS z FROM data) sub;

dialect_traps.construct: STDDEV in DuckDB vs other dialects
dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: STDDEV_POP (population) and STDDEV_SAMP (sample) — use STDDEV_SAMP for samples. Both are supported.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: STDDEV is alias for STDDEV_SAMP. Population STDDEV uses STDDEV_POP.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: STDDEV and VARIANCE are sample by default; use STDDEV_POP for population.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Explicitly use STDDEV_SAMP or STDDEV_POP to avoid ambiguity. In queries, comment which is used: -- STDDEV_SAMP = sample std dev

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: Nested COALESCE(NULLIF(...), fallback) works cleanly — null-handling is robust.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same — PostgreSQL handles nested NULLIF/COALESCE well.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same, but BigQuery’s strict type checking may require explicit CAST to FLOAT.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Always use COALESCE(NULLIF(...), fallback) for safe division or scaling to avoid NULL propagation. Test edge cases: data with 0 variance.

choose_this_when: user needs to combine 2–5 metrics into one segment score
business has clear weightings (e.g., 'recency matters twice as much as spend')
normalization is required due to different scales
scoring must be transparent and auditable (unlike ML models)

choose_alternative_when.condition: metrics conflict (e.g., high cost but low margin) — user wants trade-off analysis
choose_alternative_when.why: Regression coefficients reveal true drivers; composite scores hide trade-offs

choose_alternative_when.condition: weights should adapt to data — e.g., 'most predictive metrics' change quarterly
choose_alternative_when.why: Dynamic thresholds adjust cutoffs; composite scores assume fixed weights

choose_alternative_when.condition: only one metric matters — other dimensions are noise
choose_alternative_when.why: Composite scoring adds unnecessary complexity; one metric + thresholds is simpler

edge_cases.scenario: Zero standard deviation in one dimension
edge_cases.what_happens: z-score division by zero returns NULL — the whole score becomes NULL.
edge_cases.handling: Add NULLIF(stddev, 0) or use IQR scaling: (value - median) / NULLIF(IQR, 0).

edge_cases.scenario: Weights sum to >1 or <1 — user prefers raw score magnitude to matter
edge_cases.what_happens: Score is scaled, making thresholds arbitrary. A '100' score could mean 50–200 depending on weights.
edge_cases.handling: Normalize weights to sum to 1: SELECT weight / SUM(weight) OVER () FROM weights_table.

edge_cases.scenario: Outliers cause over-scoring — top 1% dominates tiers
edge_cases.what_happens: Top tier is 1–2 customers; most customers appear 'average'. Segments lose differentiation.
edge_cases.handling: Winsorize data: cap z-scores at ±3 or use IQR-based scaling instead of z-score.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) for stats computation + O(n log n) for sorting if ranking
performance_notes.when_slow: Large datasets (>10M rows) with many dimensions — stats require full scans.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-compute stats in a CTE or materialized view. For frequent scoring, store z-scores in a lookup table. Use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT and APPROX_PERCENTILES if exactness isn’t required.

title: Cohort Assignment by First Event
when_to_use: user wants to assign users to cohorts based on first sign-up or first purchase date
building cohort retention tables (e.g., 'all users who signed up in week 1')
analyzing cohorts by acquisition channel or geography
segmenting by first touch (e.g., first marketing source) for channel attribution
calculating cohort lifetime metrics (e.g., revenue in 90 days after acquisition)
when_not_to_use: when cohorts should be defined by behavior, not time (e.g., 'users who watched video') — use event sequence detection instead
when user IDs are unreliable and first-touch attribution is noisy — use probabilistic attribution models
for real-time cohort assignment — use streaming tools instead of SQL batch processing
when cohort definitions change rapidly — static SQL cohorts require re-running queries
for anonymous users where first event lacks identity — use device fingerprinting or hashed IDs

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Cohort assignment uses ROW_NUMBER to find the first event per user (e.g., first order date), assigns them to a cohort period (e.g., week, month), and links all future events to that cohort. It enables retention analysis: 'How many users signed up in Jan 2024 are still active in June?'
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine assigning every user to the month they first visited your site. All future activity is tagged with that cohort — like a 'birth certificate'. You then track how each cohort ages over time.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: The cohort key must be deterministic — use DATE_TRUNC(signup_date, 'month') or channel, not exact timestamp. ORDER BY must be stable (e.g., created_at DESC, then id DESC) to avoid flickering assignments.

template.minimal:
-- Assign users to monthly cohorts based on first order
WITH first_orders AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY 1
),
cohorted AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    first_order_date,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', first_order_date) AS cohort_month
  FROM first_orders
)
SELECT * FROM cohorted;
template.realistic:
-- Assign customers to weekly cohorts based on first purchase, then calculate retention
WITH first_orders AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY 1
),
cohorted AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    first_order_date,
    DATE_TRUNC('week', first_order_date) AS cohort_week,
    EXTRACT(YEAR FROM first_order_date) AS cohort_year,
    EXTRACT(WEEK FROM first_order_date) AS cohort_week_num
  FROM first_orders
),
-- Calculate active weeks per cohort
user_activity AS (
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('week', o.order_date) AS activity_week
  FROM orders o
  JOIN cohorted c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
-- Join with cohort to get retention week
with_retention AS (
  SELECT
    c.cohort_week,
    c.cohort_year,
    c.cohort_week_num,
    a.activity_week,
    DATE_TRUNC('week', a.activity_week) - INTERVAL '1 day' + '1 day'::interval - c.cohort_week AS weeks_since_cohort
  FROM cohorted c
  LEFT JOIN user_activity a ON c.customer_id = a.customer_id
  WHERE c.cohort_year = 2024 -- or dynamic
)
SELECT
  cohort_week,
  COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS cohort_size,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN wr.weeks_since_cohort = 0 THEN c.customer_id END) AS active_week_0,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN wr.weeks_since_cohort = 1 THEN c.customer_id END) AS active_week_1,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN wr.weeks_since_cohort = 2 THEN c.customer_id END) AS active_week_2
FROM cohorted c
LEFT JOIN with_retention wr ON c.customer_id = wr.customer_id
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY cohort_week;
template.composable:
-- Cohort assignment + retention matrix (combines seg_cohort_assignment + ts_cohort_retention)
-- Composes: seg_cohort_assignment + ts_cohort_retention
WITH first_orders AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY 1
),
cohorted AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', first_order_date) AS cohort_month
  FROM first_orders
),
user_activity AS (
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS activity_month
  FROM orders o
  JOIN cohorted c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
-- Calculate retention per cohort
retention AS (
  SELECT
    c.cohort_month,
    c.customer_id,
    MIN(a.activity_month) AS first_activity,
    MAX(a.activity_month) AS last_activity,
    COUNT(DISTINCT a.activity_month) AS active_months
  FROM cohorted c
  LEFT JOIN user_activity a ON c.customer_id = a.customer_id
  GROUP BY 1, 2
)
SELECT
  cohort_month,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN active_months >= 1 THEN customer_id END) AS month_1_retention,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN active_months >= 3 THEN customer_id END) AS month_3_retention,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN active_months >= 6 THEN customer_id END) AS month_6_retention
FROM retention
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY cohort_month

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using MAX(order_date) instead of MIN for cohort assignment
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Assigning users to their *last* order date places them in the cohort of their final activity, not acquisition. A user who signs up in January but orders in December is grouped with December’s cohort — not January’s.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Cohort sizes shrink over time; retention curves look like churn when it’s actually assignment error.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT user_id, MAX(order_date) AS cohort_date FROM orders GROUP BY 1;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT user_id, MIN(order_date) AS cohort_date FROM orders GROUP BY 1;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY user_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
SELECT user_id, MIN(order_date), MAX(order_date) FROM orders GROUP BY 1 LIMIT 5;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Not handling users with no orders (e.g., only page views)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Cohort assignment only includes users with orders, excluding sign-ups with no purchases — retention analysis is biased toward buyers.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Cohort sizes drop significantly after filtering; 'acquisition' cohort is actually 'first purchase' cohort.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT user_id, MIN(order_date) AS cohort_date FROM orders GROUP BY 1;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT u.user_id, COALESCE(MIN(o.order_date), u.signup_date) AS cohort_date FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id GROUP BY 1;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE signup_date IS NOT NULL;
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FROM (above query);

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using ORDER BY event_time without tiebreaker — flickering assignments
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If a user has two events at the same timestamp, MIN(order_date) is stable, but ROW_NUMBER without tiebreaker picks arbitrary rows — assignments flicker between runs.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Repeated queries return different cohort sizes or assignments for edge cases.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT user_id, order_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY order_date) AS rn FROM orders;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT user_id, order_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY order_date, id) AS rn FROM orders;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE order_date IN (SELECT MIN(order_date) FROM orders GROUP BY user_id) GROUP BY 1 HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DATE_TRUNC('week', date) uses ISO week (Monday start). For fiscal weeks, custom logic needed.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same — ISO week by default. Postgres supports WEEK with ISO standard.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: DATE_TRUNC(date, WEEK(MONDAY)) matches DuckDB. BigQuery also supports WEEK(SUNDAY) if needed.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Explicitly document the week convention. For non-ISO weeks, use CASE WHEN to adjust: DATE_TRUNC('month', date + INTERVAL 'N days').

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: COALESCE(MIN(order_date), signup_date) works — MIN returns NULL for no orders, COALESCE falls back to signup_date.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same — COALESCE is standard and robust.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same, but BigQuery’s strict NULL handling may require COALESCE to be typed explicitly: COALESCE(CAST(MIN(order_date) AS DATE), signup_date).
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Cast all fallback fields to DATE explicitly: COALESCE(CAST(MIN(order_date) AS DATE), CAST(signup_date AS DATE)).

choose_this_when: user asks 'which cohort does this user belong to?'
building cohort retention tables (e.g., 'sign-up cohort' → 30-day retention)
segmenting by acquisition time (e.g., 'users who signed up in Q1 2024')
analyzing behavior *since* acquisition (e.g., revenue in first 90 days)

choose_alternative_when.condition: cohort should be defined by behavior, not time (e.g., 'users who watched tutorial')
choose_alternative_when.why: Event sequence detection identifies behavioral milestones; time-based cohorts ignore behavior

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants dynamic cohort definitions (e.g., 'top 10% of users')
choose_alternative_when.why: Dynamic thresholds rebaseline cohorts each period; static time cohorts don’t

choose_alternative_when.condition: cohort key is channel or device, not time
choose_alternative_when.why: Channel-based segmentation is better expressed as CASE WHEN on source fields

edge_cases.scenario: User signs up but never completes an order
edge_cases.what_happens: Cohort assignment falls back to signup_date — user is included but may be classified as 'no activity' if not tracked.
edge_cases.handling: Include signup_date in the first event calculation: COALESCE(MIN(order_date), signup_date). Tag them as 'active' only if they have page views or other events.

edge_cases.scenario: Timezone differences — signup date in UTC vs local time
edge_cases.what_happens: A user signing up at 11 PM UTC on Dec 31 might be in cohort Jan 1 if local timezone is UTC+2 — month assignment shifts.
edge_cases.handling: Standardize to a single timezone before DATE_TRUNC: DATE_TRUNC('month', signup_date AT TIME ZONE 'America/New_York'). Document the timezone used.

edge_cases.scenario: First event is NULL or ambiguous (e.g., multiple channels)
edge_cases.what_happens: First-touch attribution may pick a less significant channel, distorting cohort quality.
edge_cases.handling: Define 'first event' business rules explicitly: e.g., 'first channel with >$0 spend' or 'first click after sign-up'. Filter first_events with CASE WHEN.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n log n) for MIN/ORDER BY + O(n) for join/retention
performance_notes.when_slow: Large user base (>10M users) with many events — MIN per user is expensive. Retention joins are O(n^2) without filtering.
performance_notes.optimization hint: Pre-filter to relevant date range before cohort assignment. Pre-aggregate events to first order per user in CTEs. Use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT for large cohorts. Index on user_id, order_date.
